diagrams.title: Singly Linked List Structure
diagrams.description: Shows the basic structure of a singly linked list with nodes containing data and next pointers, demonstrating unidirectional traversal from head to tail ending with NULL.
elements: 3 rectangular nodes each containing a data value (e.g., 10, 20, 30)
arrow from each node pointing right to the next node
arrow from last node pointing to 'NULL' indicator
diagrams.purpose: Explains the fundamental structure of singly linked lists, showing how each node only knows about the next node, enabling understanding of traversal, insertion at head/tail, and the concept of NULL termination.

diagrams.title: Doubly Linked List Structure
diagrams.description: Illustrates a doubly linked list showing bidirectional connections with prev and next pointers in each node, demonstrating forward and backward traversal capabilities.
elements: head pointer labeled 'HEAD' and tail pointer labeled 'TAIL'
3 rectangular nodes each split into three sections: prev pointer, data value, next pointer
NULL indicator on far left and far right
diagrams.purpose: Demonstrates the bidirectional nature of doubly linked lists, illustrating how each node maintains both forward and backward references, which helps understand advantages over singly linked lists for reverse traversal and efficient deletions.

diagrams.title: Circular Linked List Variants
diagrams.description: Shows both singly circular and doubly circular linked list arrangements, with the last node connecting back to the first node, eliminating the NULL termination.
elements: two separate circular arrangements: one for singly circular, one for doubly circular
singly circular: 4 nodes in a circle with unidirectional arrows connecting all nodes back to start
doubly circular: 4 nodes in a circle with bidirectional arrows connecting all nodes in a complete loop
head pointer pointing to first node in each variant
no NULL indicators (key difference from linear lists)
diagrams.purpose: Explains the circular nature of circular linked lists where the last node points back to the first, showing the difference between singly and doubly circular variants and their use cases in round-robin scheduling and continuous data flows.
